metadata.title: Build a Pipeline Contract Resilience Audit with Claude
metadata.purpose: Show students in Nina Harris' Branding and AI course how to build a dependency audit tool that maps each external service as a contract with risk ratings, flags single-dependency failure modes like the Apollo/Reddit case, and writes degraded-mode fallback designs.
metadata.audience: Students and practitioners in a university branding course with an AI component
sources: Branding and AI, Chapter 21 — Pipelines and Workflow (Nina Harris)

skin_warnings: B00: palette=claude but the cold open is 'ClaudeComposerAsk916' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
END: palette=claude but the outro is 'un-annotated' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

metadata.reformat: center-cut, focus-aware; pantry/<bid>-916.* is the human replacement slot; -916 overrides honored

beats.narration_text: Ciao — this is Liam, in for Bear. For Nina Harris' course, Branding and AI, here is the question we are running today: Apollo was not a bad pipeline — it was excellent. What killed it was not the code. It was the contract. Reddit changed its API terms and Apollo died. We are building the audit that maps every contract before the day it changes.

props.command: Audit these external services for contract risk: [SERVICE LIST]. For each: shape (fixed/versioned), cost model, rate limit, ToS governance. Rate dependency: LOW/MEDIUM/HIGH. Flag single-dependency failures. Write degraded-mode designs for top 2 HIGH-risk services.
output: Reddit API: HIGH across all four fields
Single-dependency flag: YES — product fails completely
Degraded mode: read-only cached content, no new ingestion
Stripe, OpenAI: mapped with risk ratings

beats.narration_text: Here is what breaks without this audit. You build an excellent product on top of someone else's API. The upstream party changes its terms. The cost structure changes, the rate limits change, the ToS changes. Damage flows downhill. Reddit absorbed diffuse reputational spread. Apollo absorbed concentrated product-killing damage. The asymmetry is structural — the smallest, most dependent party always absorbs everything.

props.sparkLine: The smallest, most dependent party absorbs everything.

beats.narration_text: The theory from Chapter Twenty-One. Every external service is a chain of contracts with four fields: Shape — is the API versioned or fixed? Cost — is pricing fixed, usage-based, or ad valorem? Rate limit — generous, constrained, or restrictive? Terms of Service governance — stable open terms or discretionary and frequently revised? The three survival disciplines: map every contract, classify every dependency risk, and design a degraded mode before the service breaks. Chapter Twenty-One names the Apollo asymmetry: the builder's job is to survive the day the upstream party changes its terms.

The centerpiece: this is the audit table for a product with Reddit API as a core dependency. Service rows: Stripe, Reddit, OpenAI, Google Maps. Four contract columns: Shape, Cost, Rate Limit, ToS Governance. The Reddit row lights terracotta across all four fields — HIGH risk on every dimension. The single-dependency badge fires: this product fails completely when this contract breaks. The degraded mode for Reddit: read-only cached content with no new post ingestion, surfaced as a clear user-facing message rather than a broken product. Source: Branding and AI, Nina Harris, Chapter Twenty-One.

Honesty about what the audit does not do. A degraded mode design is not a substitute for contractual negotiation where possible. For high-revenue products, negotiating rate caps or enterprise API agreements is preferable to designing around a failure that negotiation could prevent. The audit tells you where to invest in negotiation — not just where to build the fallback.

artifactLines: A degraded-mode design is not a substitute for negotiating better terms.
For high-revenue products, enterprise API agreements beat designing around failure.
The audit identifies where negotiation should happen — not just where to build fallbacks.
Some single dependencies cannot be replaced — name them explicitly as accepted risks.

beats.narration_text: Let us recap with Claude. Pipeline Contract Resilience Audit. Four lines.

artifactLines: Every external service is a contract with Shape, Cost, Rate, and ToS fields.
Damage flows to the smallest, most dependent party when a contract breaks.
Single-dependency flags name the failure modes that kill products, not just slow them.
The degraded mode design is the builder's insurance before the upstream party decides.

beats.narration_text: That's the short version. The full video also covers pipeline contract audit — watch Build a Pipeline Contract Resilience Audit with Claude for the whole story. The link is right below.
